questions.question: Explain the primary difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling. Why is this distinction important for an operating system?
keyPoints: Non-preemptive scheduling allows a process to run until it voluntarily gives up the CPU (e.g., terminates or blocks for I/O).
Preemptive scheduling allows the operating system to interrupt a running process and forcibly reassign the CPU to another process.
The distinction is critical for system responsiveness and ensuring fair access to the CPU among multiple processes, especially for time-sharing systems.

questions.question: List and briefly define four common scheduling criteria used to evaluate CPU scheduling algorithms.
keyPoints: CPU Utilization: The percentage of time the CPU is busy.
Throughput: The number of processes completed per unit time.
Turnaround Time: The total time from submission of a process to its completion.
Waiting Time: The total amount of time a process spends waiting in the ready queue.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the scheduling criteria optimization goals for a batch processing system versus an interactive time-sharing system. Justify why these different goals are appropriate for each environment.
keyPoints: Batch systems typically aim to maximize throughput and CPU utilization, as the primary goal is to complete as many jobs as possible with minimal resource idle time.
Time-sharing systems prioritize minimizing response time and ensuring fairness to provide a responsive and predictable experience for interactive users.
The justification lies in the user expectation: batch jobs are not interactive and benefit from high efficiency, while interactive users require immediate feedback from the system.
Waiting time and turnaround time are often secondary in time-sharing, whereas they are critical metrics for evaluating batch job performance.
